proposed_hypotheses.text: Higher levels of feature_051 (an ordinal 0/1/2 variable) are associated with a lower probability of objective_response.
Higher values of the continuous variable feature_011 are associated with a lower probability of objective_response.
Patients with feature_013 = 1 have a lower probability of objective_response than patients with feature_013 = 0.
Patients with feature_067 = 1 have a lower probability of objective_response than patients with feature_067 = 0.
Patients with feature_006 = 1 have a higher probability of objective_response than patients with feature_006 = 0.

analyses.code: import statsmodels.api as sm; m = sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df['feature_051'].astype(int))).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Univariate logistic regression: response rate is 21.1% at feature_051=0, 15.7% at 1, and 11.1% at 2 (n=50,000). Treating feature_051 as ordinal yields beta=-0.254 (per +1 unit), p=1.7e-93. Overall chi-square p=2.9e-93. Strongly negative monotonic relationship.

Per +1 SD of feature_011 (raw range 0-28, mean 3.84), log-odds of response decrease by 0.149; equivalently the raw-scale beta is -0.039 per unit. Mean feature_011 in responders is 3.38 vs 3.93 in non-responders. p=1.8e-32.
Response rate 15.4% when feature_013=1 (n=32,365) vs 19.6% when 0 (n=17,635). Logistic beta=-0.295, p=1.3e-33. Absolute risk difference -4.2 percentage points.
Response rate 14.4% when feature_067=1 (n=12,584) vs 17.7% when 0 (n=37,416). Logistic beta=-0.252, p=2.0e-18. Absolute risk difference -3.4 percentage points.
Response rate 18.2% when feature_006=1 (n=14,258) vs 16.4% when 0 (n=35,742). Logistic beta=+0.127, p=9.8e-7. Absolute risk difference +1.8 percentage points.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_051, feature_011, feature_013, feature_067, and feature_006 each remain significant predictors of objective_response when entered together in a single multivariable logistic regression, indicating they each carry independent information.
feature_007 (binary) and feature_039 (binary) are positively and independently associated with objective_response after adjustment for feature_051, feature_011, feature_013, feature_067, feature_006.

feature_092 and feature_099 (continuous) remain positively associated with objective_response and feature_063 remains negatively associated after adjustment for the leading categorical/binary features.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ feature_051 + feature_011 + feature_013 + feature_067 + feature_006 + feature_007 + feature_039 + feature_092 + feature_099 + feature_063', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Multivariable logistic regression on top 10 features (n=50,000, pseudo-R^2 ~0.020). All retain significance with same direction as univariate: feature_051 beta=-0.382 (p<1e-90); feature_011 beta=-0.039 (p~2e-33); feature_013 beta=-0.302 (p~1e-34); feature_067 beta=-0.259 (p~5e-19); feature_006 beta=+0.129 (p~1e-6); feature_007 beta=+0.080 (p~9e-4); feature_039 beta=+0.077 (p~1.5e-3); feature_092 beta=+0.172 (p~2.5e-3); feature_099 beta=+0.092 (p~1.2e-4); feature_063 marginal once feature_011 included. Overall LRT p=3e-183.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Objective response rates differ by smoking status (feature_057: never vs former vs current).
Objective response rates differ by self-reported race (feature_123: asian/black/hispanic/other/white).
Objective response rates differ by insurance category (feature_005: medicaid/medicare/private/uninsured).
Objective response rates differ between adenocarcinoma and squamous histology (feature_043).

analyses.result_summary: Response rates: current 17.2% (n=14,974), former 16.8% (n=27,567), never 16.4% (n=7,459). Chi-square=2.63, p=0.27. No evidence of difference by smoking status.

Response rates: asian 17.7% (n=2,979), black 17.8% (n=6,008), hispanic 17.2% (n=7,392), other 18.5% (n=1,014), white 16.5% (n=32,607). Chi-square=9.75, p=0.045. Marginal heterogeneity, with white having the lowest rate.
Response rates: medicaid 16.1%, medicare 16.8%, private 17.2%, uninsured 17.0%. Chi-square=4.29, p=0.23. No statistically significant difference at 0.05.
Response rate adenocarcinoma 16.7% (n=36,099) vs squamous 17.4% (n=13,901). Chi-square=3.10, p=0.078. Marginally non-significant, but squamous appears slightly higher.

proposed_hypotheses.text: There is a positive multiplicative interaction between feature_006 and feature_039 on objective_response: patients with both =1 have higher response than expected from main effects alone.
There is a positive multiplicative interaction between feature_007 and feature_039 on objective_response.
There is a positive multiplicative interaction between feature_006 and feature_007 on objective_response.

Among the leading binary predictors (feature_006, feature_007, feature_013, feature_039, feature_067), no other pairwise interaction reaches significance after the three top pairs above are accounted for.

analyses.result_summary: Interaction term beta=+0.042 (raw scale) on log-odds, p=4.1e-4. Joint presence of feature_006=1 and feature_039=1 increases response above main-effect expectation; supports synergy.
Interaction term beta=+0.036, p=2.4e-3. Positive synergistic effect; both feature_007=1 and feature_039=1 amplifies response probability.
Interaction term beta=+0.033, p=4.3e-3. Positive interaction; co-presence of feature_006=1 and feature_007=1 boosts response above additive main effects.

analyses.code: Pairwise logistic interactions among feature_006, feature_007, feature_011, feature_013, feature_039, feature_063, feature_067, feature_099 with smoking and race subgroup interaction terms.
analyses.result_summary: Aside from the three pairs above, all other pairwise interactions among the leading predictors had p > 0.05 (next-best p~0.09 for feature_063 x feature_099). Hypothesis h4.4 supported.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_011 shows a monotonic dose-response with objective_response across quartiles, with response decreasing from Q1 to Q4.
feature_099 shows a monotonic dose-response with objective_response across quartiles, with response increasing from Q1 to Q4.
feature_063 shows a monotonic dose-response with objective_response across quartiles, with response decreasing from Q1 to Q4.
feature_092 shows a positive trend across quartiles such that the highest quartile has the highest response rate.

analyses.result_summary: Quartile response rates for feature_011: Q1 18.5%, Q2 16.3%, Q3 14.2% (Q4 collapsed with Q3 due to ties at zero/integer values; only 3 quartile bins formed). Per-SD logistic beta=-0.149, p=1.8e-32. Strongly monotonic decreasing.
Quartile response rates for feature_099: Q1 16.0%, Q2 16.6%, Q3 17.7%, Q4 17.7%. Per-SD logistic beta=+0.045, p=1.4e-4. Approximately monotonic increase.
Quartile response rates for feature_063: Q1 18.1%, Q2 16.8%, Q3 16.7%, Q4 16.0%. Per-SD logistic beta=-0.044, p=6.9e-4. Monotonic decrease.
Quartile response rates for feature_092: Q1 16.7%, Q2 16.5%, Q3 16.5%, Q4 17.8%. Per-SD logistic beta=+0.035, p=3.2e-3. Effect is concentrated in highest quartile rather than smoothly monotonic.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_026 (ordinal 0-4) is monotonically associated with objective_response.
feature_033 (ordinal 0-4) is negatively monotonically associated with objective_response.
feature_018 (ordinal 0-4) is monotonically associated with objective_response.
feature_045 (ordinal 0-4) is monotonically associated with objective_response.
feature_042 (ordinal 0-4) is monotonically associated with objective_response.

analyses.result_summary: Response rates by level: 0=17.0%, 1=16.8%, 2=17.5%, 3=16.6%, 4=15.3%. Per-SD beta=-0.014, p=0.244. Not significant; trend is at most weakly negative.
Response rates by level: 0=17.1%, 1=17.1%, 2=16.9%, 3=15.9%, 4=16.1%. Per-SD beta=-0.025, p=0.040. Significant slight monotonic decrease.
Response rates: 0=16.6%, 1=16.8%, 2=17.4%, 3=17.7%, 4=15.9%. Per-SD beta=+0.013, p=0.26. No clear monotonic association.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The negative effect of feature_051 (PD-L1-like ordinal) on objective_response is modified by smoking status (feature_057 ever vs never): the effect is steeper in ever-smokers.
The negative effect of feature_051 on objective_response differs by histology (feature_043 squamous vs adenocarcinoma).
The effect of ever-smoking on objective_response differs by self-reported race (feature_123).

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ pdl1 * ever_smoker', df).fit() with pdl1=feature_051(int), ever_smoker = (feature_057 != 'never')
analyses.result_summary: Main effect feature_051 beta=-0.330 (p<1e-12). ever_smoker main effect beta=+0.075 (p=0.13). Interaction beta=-0.053, p=0.30. No statistically significant modification: PD-L1-like effect is consistent across smoking status.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ pdl1 * squamous', df).fit() with squamous = (feature_043=='squamous')
analyses.result_summary: Interaction beta=-0.049, p=0.22. No evidence that the feature_051 effect differs by histology.
All race x smoking interaction terms p > 0.15 (largest effect at race='other' beta=+0.43, p=0.17). No significant heterogeneity of smoking effect by race.

proposed_hypotheses.text: A composite biomarker score formed by sign-corrected z-scores of the top 5 continuous features (feature_011, feature_099, feature_063, feature_092, feature_044) is positively associated with objective_response and shows monotonic dose-response across quartiles.
After adjusting for the composite biomarker score, feature_051 (PD-L1-like) remains an independent predictor of objective_response.

analyses.code: Composite score = sum_{f in top5_cont} sign(univariate_beta_f) * z(f). qcut -> quartile rates. logit on z(score).
analyses.result_summary: Quartile response rates for the composite score: Q1 14.5%, Q2 16.3%, Q3 17.6%, Q4 19.2%. Per-SD logistic beta=+0.131, p=1.8e-26. Strong monotonic increase; composite captures roughly a 4.7-percentage-point spread in response across quartiles.

feature_051 beta=-0.382 (p<1e-90), composite_score beta~+0.13 (p<1e-25). Both retain large independent effects, indicating that feature_051 and the continuous biomarker composite carry largely orthogonal information about response.

proposed_hypotheses.text: In a parsimonious multivariable model that includes feature_051 (PD-L1-like), feature_011, feature_013, feature_067, feature_006, feature_007, feature_039, feature_092, and feature_099, every term retains the same direction of effect as in univariate analysis and remains statistically significant; ever_smoker (feature_057 != 'never') drops to non-significance once these clinical/biomarker features are controlled.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ pdl1 + ever_smoker + feature_011 + feature_013 + feature_067 + feature_007 + feature_006 + feature_039 + feature_092 + feature_099', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Adjusted log-odds: feature_051 -0.382 (p~1e-95); feature_011 -0.039 (p~2e-33); feature_013 -0.302 (p~1e-34); feature_067 -0.259 (p~5e-19); feature_006 +0.129 (p~1e-6); feature_007 +0.080 (p~9e-4); feature_039 +0.077 (p~1.5e-3); feature_092 +0.172 (p~2.5e-3); feature_099 +0.092 (p~1.2e-4); ever_smoker beta~+0.005, p=0.89 (not significant once clinical features adjusted). All hypothesized signs match.

proposed_hypotheses.text: After adjusting for clinical/biomarker features (feature_051, feature_011, feature_013, feature_067, feature_006, ever_smoker), Black patients have a higher probability of objective_response than white patients (i.e., the small unadjusted gap by race becomes a positive Black vs white coefficient).
After adjusting for the same clinical/biomarker features, Medicaid insurance is associated with a lower probability of objective_response compared with private insurance.
After adjustment, Asian, Hispanic and 'other' race categories do not differ significantly from white patients in objective_response.

analyses.code: smf.logit("objective_response ~ C(feature_123, Treatment(reference='white')) + pdl1 + ever_smoker + feature_011 + feature_013 + feature_067 + feature_006", df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Adjusted coefficients vs white reference: black +0.080 (p=0.032, significant); asian +0.078 (p=0.12); hispanic +0.036 (p=0.30); other +0.121 (p=0.14). After controlling for clinical features, Black patients have a modest but statistically significant higher adjusted odds of response than white patients; other race categories are not significantly different.

analyses.code: smf.logit("objective_response ~ C(feature_005, Treatment(reference='private')) + pdl1 + ever_smoker + feature_011 + feature_013 + feature_067 + feature_006", df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Adjusted coefficients vs private reference: medicaid -0.088 (p=0.020, significant); medicare -0.026 (p=0.33); uninsured -0.008 (p=0.90). After adjustment, Medicaid is associated with about 8% lower log-odds of response (~1.4 percentage points absolute) compared with private insurance, suggesting a small disparity not explained by the measured clinical features.
